speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Deborah Cutler, Los Angeles. Language-- English. Second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were waiting for a smuggler. And I forgot to mention-- and it was a few years back, 1933. I was eight years old. My grandparents, my mother's father and mother, lived in Berlin, too. And they were very religious, I mean, observant and religious. When he goes to the temple, you're not allowed to carry anything. So I was the, the grandchild, the oldest grandchild, the boy. So he took me to the synagogue to carry his tallit, you know, the shawl, the prayer shawl. And we came from the-- this was a Friday night-- we came from the synagogue. And while we were walking, it was-- it was dark. The street was not very, very-- there was not very many people on it. But from far, we saw two SS men on each side of the sidewalk walking, with the black boots, with the black uniforms, and the swastika on the-- with the whole uniform, two on each side of the sidewalk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my grandfather, when he saw this, he took me on his hand. And he walked down from the sidewalk in the street, not to bother them so they can pass. At this moment, both-- from both sides, the four guys approached, came to my grandfather, and pulled out the night sticks and started beating him up. He was severely beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started screaming and crying until some neighbors came out and called the number. And then he was taken to the hospital. So this was one episode I want to mention. This was in 1933. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS] To continue, on our trip to-- our trip-- our [LAUGHS] exodus from Germany to Belgium, we walked, like I said, the whole night. We were waiting there for the smuggler to come back with the car. 3 o'clock-- and it's 4 o'clock. And it's 5 o'clock. Nobody showed up. It's raining very strong. And it is cold. We are soaked from top to bottom, soaked, cold, shivering. We were so cold that we all got together. We, we came all together to, to get warmed up a little bit. And it was-- it started to get light. And it must have been 8:00 in the morning because in the winter, it doesn't get so early light in Europe. And all of a sudden-- yeah, in the meantime, a few cars went by. It was a highway. A few cars went by. Nobody stopped. And all of a sudden, one car came and stopped and blinked the lights. We were in heaven. We, we jumped. We jumped out from, from behind the woods and run to the-- to the highway. And there was a five-seater car. I mean, five people could go in. And we were seven or eight with me. I was a, a young-- I was a bar mitzvah boy. Anyway, we all squeezed in in this car. And he drove us to Antwerp. The smuggler told us that he couldn't start the car. He had a, a, a, a engine problem. He had to borrow a car from a friend. Anyway, this is why it took so long. But we thought that we were had for, you know, that we paid him, and he left us in the woods. But it wasn't like this. He picked us up. And we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two or three hours later, we arrived in Antwerp, a certain place where my father was already waiting with other people who, who were from the other families. And the first thing, of course, where's Mania and Willy? So my mother cried and told him what happened. So he says, don't worry, they, they will-- they will come, too. As it happens, we found out later my, my sister-- they missed the train. There were too many people. And they were headed back.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train left. And they were stranded on the-- on the-- I don't know what you call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Platform.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the platform, yeah, on the platform from the train station. So my sister said, let's wait for the next train. And I put you in the train to Belgium straight. You're a little boy. Maybe nobody will ask you nothing, you know. When the police comes in the train, they ask passport and visas to grown-ups. Children, usually, they don't ask. So that's what she did. And she put him in the train. And, and, and he arrived in the-- in the city, in Antwerp. And he didn't have any papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were people asking him, where are you going? He says, Antwerp. And then they, they notified the, the, the authorities. And they called into Antwerp and to the Jewish Committee. And my father found out that my brother was on the way to Antwerp by himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my sister is stuck in Germany. And we always got correspondence through the Jewish Committee. There were always-- Jewish Committee were helping Jewish refugees, and things like this. I don't know till today how they got in touch. My sister got in touch with my parents. And they, they, they sent somebody to Germany. She was stuck on the German border-- sent somebody to smuggle her in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- and the man came with a bicycle and another bicycle. Luckily, my, my sister knew how to, to ride a bicycle. So the smuggler came with the bicycle for her. And they started driving, riding the bicycle, in from Germany over to the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they got into a big field of maneuver. The German Army were just maneuvering. And they were caught by the German military police. And the smuggler was a Belgian. And he, he talked. He says, we are Belge. We, we took a little ride on the bike. And we must have crossed. And the German, this soldier, was nice enough so that, well, Belgium, you have to go this way. So they drove both into Belgium. And she came to first city. And they sent a ticket to come to Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were reunited. I mean, this was like a thing from, from heaven. I don't know how, how this all combination worked. But we were all four, all five, again back together in Antwerp. So my father started to look for a little apartment. We were in a hotel room what he got from the Jewish Committee-- put us in a hotel until we can find an apartment. And my dad found an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we all moved in there. It was-- it was a store, an empty store what could never be rented. So they painted the, the, the show windows white with paint. And the back was two rooms and a toilet. And we had an apartment. And we stayed there for a while. And my father-- you know, you have to make a living. And he did not know Flemish. This is the language they talk in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to the bourse-- to the bourse-- it's a bourse where they deal with diamonds. Especially Belgium, Antwerp, was very well known for the diamond bourse. And what does a [YIDDISH] do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he bought cigarettes wholesale. And he sold them to the dealers there, retail. And he made a little living, you know, brought in some money here, some money there. And that a little bit and the bourse-- and we were surviving. My sister started a job as a nanny. She was at this time-- excuse me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 13, she was 16, 16 years old-- as a babysitter in a Jewish family. She-- they, they took her in. And she was guarding a little girl. And she was fed. And so it was easy on us to, to, to survive. Excuse me. [DRINKS] My brother and I, we were young. We were always happy-go-lucky. We were always optimistic. This we had from our parents. We never saw a half glass half empty. We always saw it half full. Though we were refugees in Belgium, we survived. And we hoped that the war will be over soon, and we will go back. As it turned out, it got worse. And we stayed in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Me, myself, as a young boy, I did not fear the, the real-- how should I say? The-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] well, I, I did not have the problem with survivor. We were children. We were taken care of by our parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad was worrying for us. My mother was cooking. So we did not have any, any, any worries. We were going-- there was-- from the committee, there was a, a restaurant, a Jewish restaurant, who served meals for lunch. And we went there. We met children, also refugees. There were a lot of children from Austria and from Germany in Belgium as refugees. We went to the parks, what we never could do in Germany. And we played. And there were family who lent us roller skates, so we can play. We found out there's a big temple. My brother and I, we joined again the choir. And we were singing there. And in, and in January of 1940, was my brother's bar mitzvah in Belgium. So here we had a nice bar mitzvah for him. And we were singing in the choir. And he had a beautiful bar mitzvah. This was in Belgium. Like I said, we were happy children. We played. And then my father said, enough of the playing. You're, you're going to grow up. You have to learn something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had a friend who was a tailor. The tailor was working for the refugees. He was a refugee himself. He was working out of his house.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a little sewing machine, you know, not even electric. You pedal with your foot. And I came up there. My father presented me. He says, teach my son something to do. Like they say in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. 'Let him take a needle in the hand.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So he started to teach me about sewing. First, he gave me a needle, even without thread. And he says, here, see, you go like this. He gave me a, a-- what do you call this, a, a, thimble? Thimble.

speaker: interviewee
text: A thimble without a top. It was just a thimble. And he says, see you go like this with the needle. And very, very slightly, you touch the, the, the fabric. And then he gave me white thread on black fabric. And it shouldn't be seen on the other side. So I did it until I did it well. Then he gave me little jackets to put in the, the-- what do call the inside of the jackets, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lining?

speaker: interviewee
text: The lining-- to put in the lining, with-- already with the black thread. I was good enough to do this. And slowly, slowly-- I was miserable. All the children were playing out in the parks. And I was sitting here with a needle. I was 14 years old, sewing. I don't know for what, what was the purpose. The purpose was, he gave me five francs at the end of the week, like, it's pocket money. So I did this. And my father was selling cigarettes. My sister was a nanny. And my brother was home. He was-- my brother was always very fragile. And he developed in Belgium-- he, he got sick. He became-- he had something with the gland. And this was from undernourishment. We didn't have-- you know, we were eating. But we did not get the right food in the beginning. I was always sturdy. I was always, always a roughneck, they called me. And so he was more the guarded one by my mother. He was home. And we were all busy. We had later on a nicer little apartment, where we had already room for us-- and in a street with regular apartments. And my mother started fixing draperies in the windows. And they were talking about some carpets. We had some second-hand furniture in the house. We built up. And it was very comfortable. We had very nice, comfortable home again in Belgium. And we thought this will last forever. But it didn't. In May, 1940, the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started to occupy. The Germans were already in war with Poland and France and England. But Belgium, Holland, and Luxembourg were still free. And in 1940-- May 10, 1940-- the Germans occupy-- started to occupy Belgium, Holland, and, and Luxembourg. It started on a Friday. Hitler's movement was always on Fridays, I think. It must be one of his lucky days or whatever. He-- we, we woke up with bombardments. I mean, they bombarded Antwerp, Brussels, and all the major cities. We ran to the shelters. First, we didn't know what happened. Then, on the radio, they came out. Germany had declared-- they didn't declare war. They started to drive into Belgium and Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we are, German refugees in, in, in Belgium. What do you do? People did not know what to do. There was a lot of Jews, Belgian Jews, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Hitler once said in his speeches in Germany-- I remember it as I heard it. He says, one day, I will come to Belgium and clean up. There was a Pelican Street. Pelican Street is the major street of the diamond industry. And it was mostly in Jewish hands at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Hitler said, I will come to Belgium and clean up this Jewish nest over there. And here we hear the bombs flying in, in May of 1940. And what do you do? Where do you run?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we, we don't have anything again, you know. Again, we took a package. My mother took a suitcase, my father a suitcase. We took little packages. We closed the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went to the-- to the main, main railway station. And we're lucky because it was pandemonium, you say? I mean, it was terrible. People from all sides running to the trains. People from Brussels came to Antwerp. People from Antwerp went to Brussels. We didn't know exactly where they are. But we knew Belgium is small. And, and, and Germany was very strong. How long will it take them to occupy? So we went to the train and was lucky to get the train to Brussels. We came to Brussels. Because Antwerp-Brussels is a-- is a little train what goes every hour like a subway. But it was not underneath. It was surface. Aboveground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brussels-- we came to Brussels. And there was a long train, a real train standing there. And everybody ran. We didn't have tickets, nothing. We just ran to the train. Everybody ran to the train. We didn't know even where it's going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sat down in the train. And the train started moving. It went. And it stopped. It went because there was bombardment. It was derailed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know until today how this train could leave Brussels after those bombardments-- what the Germans did in Belgium. But the, the train moved. And we-- after a couple of days, we arrived on the border of France. And on the border of France, we saw French soldiers, British soldiers. And we cheered. And we were in the windows, Vive la France! That's the first word we learned. Everybody was shouting. The people in Brussels talk French. We just started to learn Flemish. From German to Flemish is not so difficult. And children, we learned fast. You learn very fast languages. And so, Vive la France! Vive la France! We shout, Vive la France! We were happy to go in to France. We know there's a war going on. France is a big country. There's soldiers. There's British soldiers. We will be able to be saved. The train went into France. And we were going and going. Wherever we stopped, the people, the local people, sent the train farther. We, we stopped on, on big stations. We had to be fed. There was no food on the train. So on the train stations-- it was war time-- there was Red Cross, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like, not stores, but stands who served milk and bread to the children and coffee. And later on, they knew that refugee trains are coming. And on major stations, people were received. Like when the train came, it stopped. We could go out and, and take things-- not buying, because we didn't have money, but to get food and milk and whatever people need. And a lot of time happened, that-- I know from a-- from a case, a mother was with two children in the train. And the father went out to get the milk. And the train left. And the father was gone. And, and there was no-- you could not run after the train. It was not a regular train. There was not a scheduled train who came to, to the next station. And you can say, I'll go out and wait for the next train for my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after being about eight days in this train, we arrived to the end of our line. It's really the end of the line. The train stopped, and there was no more rails. I mean, it was the end of the-- France.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is called-- this village was called Luchon, Bagneres-de-Luchon. It is-- it is a, a spa, a beautiful spa, a little village. And where the village ends, you have mountains, the Pyrenees, I mean, rocks. This village alone is about 1,200 feet already-- 1,200 meters above sea level. Going into this, we went through mountains. I mean, I never have seen mountains in my life. We were always flatlands, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came into those mountains. We looked around. It was-- we felt so, so closed in until we came to this village. And this train had about 1,200 people. Of course, they were not all Jews. There were maybe 300, 400 Jewish people on this train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- we were infiltrated. In, in France, there were, 'Fifth Column,' they called them, German parachutists. They parachuted as priests down to, to, to France and mixed in with refugees. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know what they, they could find out from refugees. But this is how they went into France as spies. And we came to this village. There were some, some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Belgian officers. And right away, they went to the French authorities there. And they said, we are Belge. But there are a lot of Jews here, refugees from, from other countries. And so, so they separated already. In France, the, the, the Christian people were taken care of from the government. And they took care of us, too. The first few weeks, they put us in hotels. And we were-- we were fed by the hotels. In the hotels, we were fed and taken care of. And after a little while, it was-- we, we arrived there May 10, May 18-- it was almost in the end of May. And in June-- in June, the French army capitulated. It meant that France is occupied, will be occupied by Germany. Here we are, in a little village in the end of the-- of the tracks. And we hear the Germans occupied France. Then it became part Vichy, part German occupation. So luckily, we were in the Vichy part of France. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were in Luchon about two or three months. And there was no radio communications. But they gave out flyers. And in the littler streets in the village, came a man with a drum. And he went from street to street drumming, and shouting out, announcement! Avis! Avis! This means 'announcement.' And then he came, 'The government of France, the, the authorities of Luchon, requires that all the Jews have to come to this place and present themselves.' OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to go to the city hall. Everybody got the paper that we are Jews and we are refugees. And also we got some cards, tickets-- for bread, oil, sugar, and, you know, things to live on because there was no food to buy in the grocery store. You had to have tickets for everything. For soap, for chocolate-- for everything you had to have tickets.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in this village. They cannot just take us and throw us out. So they gave us a paper that put in a big J, 'Juif,' 'Jew.' And they gave us some, some tickets to live-- two pounds of sugar a month, a bottle of oil a month, you know, really, the bare necessity. And as children, we received 350 gram of bread a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in this village. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my, my, my parents were looking for an apartment because we could not stay in the hotel. And right away came the Jewish Committee. It was-- I don't know from where the Jewish Committee always-- they always popped out like a mushroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people brought money. They got money from America. They got from the Joint. I don't know how it got to France during the war. But they came. And they helped. They gave money. They said, try to find an apartment. We will try to help you to pay the rent, you know. And my parents found a little apartment with the French people who had an imprimerie, a printing shop. He was printing-- they had once a week a movie. So they printed a, a colored poster-- what movie is playing, what the announcements will be, and things like this. And they rented us a little apartment, very nice people. And the, the man who, who had the imprimerie had a son who was married, had two children. He was working there with his father. But he lived about six kilometer out of this village-- from this city, Luchon-- in a village that was called Juzet-de-Luchon. This was a small village about six kilometers away from Luchon. And we got friendly with those people. And we lived in this apartment until one day, the drummer comes and announces all the Jews have to present themselves the next day on the train station. They will be taken to a different place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was nowhere to run. I mean, all mountains around and a small village. So we took our stuff again, got ready, and went to the train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was already trains like they put in for horses. This was not regular wagons. It was-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH] where they put horses and cows in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were loaded into those wagons. The train took off. And it must have taken about three or four hours later. They told us, we take you to a camp, to a working camp for the men. You will be happy there. You will have beds with white sheets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the government will care for you. Don't worry. You will be together with your family. And we have to take you there because we cannot sustain you in the village. We have to-- we don't have enough food for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ended up in a-- in a camp was called Agde. May I change the tape now?
